---
title: "Week 3 Practices (0310)"
---

## Project

1. Open the project you create in the first week. 

2. Create a new script for today's exercise and save in the script folder.


## GNOSIA (古諾希亞)

Yuri is trapped in a time loop! In each loop, there are 15 possible people who may appear. After repeating the loop many times, he begins to suspect that Yuriko appears more often than the other people. To investigate this, Yuri decides to record which people appear in each of the next 50 loops. The dataset contains:

   - `loop`: The loop number.
   - `Yuri`, `Setsu`, `Gina`, `SQ`, `Raqio`, `Stella`, `Shigemichi`, `Chipie`, `Comet`, `Jonas`,
   `Kukrushka`, `Otome`, `Sha-Ming`, `Remnan`, `Yuriko`: Each of these columns records whether the 
   person appeared in that loop, using `1` for “appeared” and `0` for “did not appear”.

Yuri wants to know: after how many loops can he reject the null hypothesis with `p < 0.0001` that Yuriko has the same appearance rate as the other people? 

Follow the instructions below to help Yuri find the answer:

0. Download the dataset `gnosia.csv`. [(right click here)](gnosia.csv){target=_blank rel="noopener"}
Then call the package `tidyverse`.

1. Read `gnosia.csv` and save it as `gnosia_raw`.

2. Because `Setsu` is also a time looper, she will appear in each loop as well. To adjust this, 
create a new dataframe called `gnosia_tidy` by removing the columns `loop`, `Yuri`, and `Setsu` in `gnosia_raw`.

::: {.callout-tip title="Note"}
In steps 3-6, we will create a function `t_test_pval()` to help us calculate the p-value from the t-test for a given loop data.
:::

3. Write a function called `t_test_pval()` that takes a dataframe `data` as input.

4. Inside `t_test_pval()`, create a new column `appear_sum` to `data` that calculates the row sum of all columns in the input dataframe.

5. Still inside `t_test_pval()`, create a new column `not_Yuriko_appear_rate`, defined as the average appearance rate of the 12 people other than `Yuriko`.

::: {.callout-tip title="Hint"}
Subtract `Yuriko` from `appear_sum`, then divide by `12`.
:::

6. Inside `t_test_pval()`, use an paired sample `t.test()` to compare `Yuriko` and `not_Yuriko_appear_rate`, then extract and return the p-value `p`.

7. Before the loop starts, create two variables:

   - `minimal_loop`, with initial value `0`.
   - `finish_index`, with initial value `0`.

8. Use a `for` loop from `2` to `50`. In each iteration:

   - Keep only the first `i` rows of `gnosia_tidy` and save it as `current_loop_data`.
   - Pass `current_loop_data` into `t_test_pval()` and save the returned value as `pval`.

9. Inside the `for` loop, check whether: `pval < 0.0001`, and `finish_index == 0`. If both conditions are true, save `i` as `minimal_loop`, and change `finish_index` to `1`.

10. Print the value `pval` in each loop.

11. After the `for` loop ends, print `minimal_loop`. What is the smallest number of loops needed for Yuri to reject the null hypothesis?

![](gnosia.gif){width="960" fig-align="center" fig-alt="Animated welcome"}
